sections.question:
1. Mayor Alper owns a retail glass shop which he operates as a sole proprietorship. He is also a licensed glazing contractor. In the normal course of his business he is called on by his customers to repair and replace broken windows, screens, entrance doors, shower doors and similar items. Such repairs and replacements are normally in the $100 or less range, but the relationship with the customers is ongoing and with certain customers amounts to $1,000 or more in a given year. These repairs and replacement orders are normally called in by agents of commercial and rental properties and require no formal proposal or bid prior to undertaking the work. Under these circumstances, does Mayor Alper have a conflict of interest such that he would be required to disqualify himself should any of his customers come before the Town Council respecting pending Town Council action?

2. As a glazing contractor, Mayor Alper submits competitive bids on a variety of projects and to a variety of builders and developers. At times, builders and developers appear before the Town Council while Mayor Alper's bids are being considered. Mayor Alper has no relationship with any given builder or developer until his bid is accepted. Once a bid is accepted, a contract is signed with the builder or developer, but it may be up to six (6) months after the contract is signed before Mayor Alper begins work and delivers material. Mayor Alper does not invoice the builder or developer for his contract charges until after work is done or materials delivered. Under these circumstances, does Mayor Alper have a conflict of interest such that he would be required to disqualify himself should any of these builders or developers come before the Town Council respecting pending Town Council action? If such a conflict of interest exists, at what point in time does it arise such that a disqualification thereafter would be required?
sections.question_synthetic: Does Mayor Alper, who operates a glass shop and glazing contractor business, have conflicts of interest requiring disqualification when his customers or builders/developers appear before the Town Council? Specifically, when do such conflicts arise given the timing of bids, contracts, and work performance?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a town attorney to the FPPC Opinions Section, not an FPPC response. Document contains no CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section. Two detailed questions regarding conflict of interest disqualification issues are clearly identified and numbered.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Mayor Alper owns a retail glass shop which he operates as a sole proprietorship. He is also a licensed glazing contractor. In the normal course of his business he is called on by his customers to repair and replace broken windows, screens, entrance doors, shower doors and similar items. Such repairs and replacements are normally in the $100 or less range, but the relationship with the customers is ongoing and with certain customers amounts to $1,000 or more in a given year. These repairs and replacement orders are normally called in by agents of commercial and rental properties and require no formal proposal or bid prior to undertaking the work. Under these circumstances, does Mayor Alper have a conflict of interest such that he would be required to disqualify himself should any of his customers come before the Town Council respecting pending Town Council action?

2. As a glazing contractor, Mayor Alper submits competitive bids on a variety of projects and to a variety of builders and developers. At times, builders and developers appear before the Town Council while Mayor Alper's bids are being considered. Mayor Alper has no relationship with any given builder or developer until his bid is accepted. Once a bid is accepted, a contract is signed with the builder or developer, but it may be up to six (6) months after the contract is signed before Mayor Alper begins work and delivers material. Mayor Alper does not invoice the builder or developer for his contract charges until after work is done or materials delivered. Under these circumstances, does Mayor Alper have a conflict of interest such that he would be required to disqualify himself should any of these builders or developers come before the Town Council respecting pending Town Council action? If such a conflict of interest exists, at what point in time does it arise such that a disqualification thereafter would be required?

embedding.summary: A town attorney requests FPPC guidance on whether Mayor Gordon Alper's ownership of a glass shop and glazing contractor business creates conflicts of interest requiring him to disqualify himself from Town Council votes involving his customers and business clients.
